problems.explanationZh: 这个题初看似乎有些困难，实际上相当直接，而且已经给出的四个数字会让它更容易。首先可以发现，直径相对的两个方格有相同的平方差。例如图中 14 的平方与 2 的平方之差是 192，16 的平方与 8 的平方之差也是 192；任何合法排列都必须满足这一点。 还要记住：两个连续整数平方的差，总是较小数的两倍再加 1；任意两个数平方的差，都等于两数之差乘以两数之和。因此 5^2 - 4^2 = (2 x 4) + 1 = 9，而 7^2 - 3^2 = (7 + 3)(7 - 3) = 40。 上面的 192 可以分成五对不同的偶因数：2 x 96、4 x 48、6 x 32、8 x 24、12 x 16。各自除以 2 后得到 1 x 48、2 x 24、3 x 16、4 x 12、6 x 8。每一对的差与和依次给出 47、49；22、26；13、19；8、16；2、14。这些就是所需数字，其中四个已经在图中给出。余下六个数可以用六种方式放置；其中一种按顺时针读为：16、2、49、22、19、8、14、47、26、13。 还有一点值得注意：在这种圆形排列中，直径相对数字的差按固定节奏增加；除 6 格圆外，最初的差为 4 和 6，后面的差由隔一个的前项加倍得到。在本例中，第一个差是 2，之后增加 4、6、8、12。若允许分数，当然可以得到无限多个解。不过这种圆中的方格数必须形如 4n + 6，也就是 6 加上 4 的倍数。

problems.promptEn: Farmer Longmore had a curious aptitude for arithmetic, and was known in his district as the ‘mathematical farmer.' The new vicar was not aware of this fact when, meeting his worthy parishioner one day in the lane, he asked him in the course of a short conversation, ‘Now, how many sheep have you altogether?' He was therefore rather surprised at Longmore's answer, which was as follows: ‘You can divide my sheep into two different parts, so that the difference between the two numbers is the same as the difference between their squares. Maybe, Mr. Parson, you will like to work out the little sum for yourself.' Can the reader say just how many sheep the farmer had? Supposing he had possessed only twenty sheep, and he divided them into the two parts 12 and 8. Now, the difference between their squares, 144 and 64, is 80. So that will not do, for 4 and 80 are certainly not the same. If you can find numbers that work out correctly, you will know exactly how many sheep Farmer Longmore owned.
problems.promptZh: 农夫朗莫尔对算术有一种好奇的天赋， 在他所在的地区被称为“数学农民”。新牧师没有意识到这一事实，有一天，他在小巷里遇见了他可敬的教区居民，并在简短的交谈中问他：“现在，你总共有多少只羊？”因此，他对朗莫尔的回答感到相当惊讶，如下：“你可以把我的羊分成两个不同的部分，这样两个数字之间的差就等于它们的平方之间的差。”也许，帕森先生，您会愿意自己算出这笔小钱。 读者能说出农夫到底有多少只羊吗？假设他只有二十只羊，他把它们分成两部分12和8。现在，它们的平方144和64之间的差是80。所以这是不行的，因为4和80肯定不一样。如果您能找到正确计算的数字，您就会确切地知道农民朗莫尔拥有多少只羊。
problems.explanation: The farmer had one sheep only! If he divided this sheep (which is best done by weight) into two parts, making one part two-thirds and the other part one-third, then the difference between these two numbers is the same as the difference between their squares—that is, one-third. Any two fractions will do if the denominator equals the sum of the two numerators.
problems.explanationEn: The farmer had one sheep only! If he divided this sheep (which is best done by weight) into two parts, making one part two-thirds and the other part one-third, then the difference between these two numbers is the same as the difference between their squares—that is, one-third. Any two fractions will do if the denominator equals the sum of the two numerators.
problems.explanationZh: 农夫只有一只羊！如果他把这只羊（最好按重量）分成两部分，一部分为三分之二，另一部分为三分之一，那么这两个数字之差就等于它们的平方之差，即三分之一。如果分母等于两个分子之和，则任何两个分数都可以。

problems.promptEn: All cannon-balls are to be piled in square pyramids, was the order issued to the regiment. This was done. Then came the further order: all pyramids are to contain a square number of balls. The major objected that a pyramid with 16 balls at the base, then 9, 4, and 1 at the top, contains 30 balls, so it needs six more balls or five fewer to be a square. The general insisted that the right number must be used. Is it really possible to obey both orders?
problems.promptZh: 团里下令：所有炮弹都要堆成四棱锥。士兵照做以后，又收到进一步命令：每个锥体中的炮弹总数必须是一个平方数。少校指出，底层 16 个、上面 9 个、再上面 4 个、顶端 1 个的锥体共有 30 个炮弹，要成为平方数就得多 6 个或少 5 个。将军坚持必须找到合适的数量。真的能同时满足这两个命令吗？
problems.explanation: The smallest workable number is 4,900 cannon-balls. Write the usual summation table: the first row is 1, 2, 3, ...; the second row gives triangular numbers; the third row gives triangular pyramids; and the fourth row gives square pyramids. Continuing this table to the 24th place gives 4,900 in the fourth row, and 4,900 = 70^2, so the balls can also be laid out as a square. In formula form, the square-pyramidal number is n(n + 1)(2n + 1)/6 = (2n^3 + 3n^2 + n)/6. For this to be a square, the non-trivial case used here is n = 24, giving 4,900.
problems.explanationEn: The smallest workable number is 4,900 cannon-balls. Write the usual summation table: the first row is 1, 2, 3, ...; the second row gives triangular numbers; the third row gives triangular pyramids; and the fourth row gives square pyramids. Continuing this table to the 24th place gives 4,900 in the fourth row, and 4,900 = 70^2, so the balls can also be laid out as a square. In formula form, the square-pyramidal number is n(n + 1)(2n + 1)/6 = (2n^3 + 3n^2 + n)/6. For this to be a square, the non-trivial case used here is n = 24, giving 4,900.
problems.explanationZh: 最小可行数量是 4,900 个炮弹。把求和表写出来：第一行是 1, 2, 3, ...；第二行是三角数；第三行是三角锥数；第四行就是四棱锥数。把表延伸到第 24 项时，第四行得到 4,900，而 4,900 = 70^2，所以这些炮弹也能在平面上摆成一个正方形。公式写法为 n(n + 1)(2n + 1)/6 = (2n^3 + 3n^2 + n)/6；本题的非平凡解取 n = 24，得到 4,900。

problems.promptEn: First, cut out a piece of paper or cardboard of the shape shown in the illustration. It will be seen at once that the proportions are simply those of a square attached to half of another similar square, divided diagonally. The puzzle is to cut it into four pieces all of precisely the same size and shape. Fig. 3. Fig. 4.
problems.promptZh: 首先，剪出一张如图所示形状的纸或纸板 图中。立刻就会看出，这些比例只是一个正方形与另一个相似正方形的一半相连的比例，对角线分开。难题是将其切成大小和形状完全相同的四块。 图3 图4
problems.explanation: The solution to this puzzle is shown in the illustration. Divide the figure up into twelve equal triangles, and it is easy to discover the directions of the cuts, as indicated by the dark lines.
problems.explanationEn: The solution to this puzzle is shown in the illustration. Divide the figure up into twelve equal triangles, and it is easy to discover the directions of the cuts, as indicated by the dark lines.
problems.explanationZh: 这个谜题的答案如图所示。将图形分成十二个相等的三角形，很容易发现切口的方向，如黑线所示。

problems.promptEn: I have often had occasion to remark on the practical utility of puzzles, arising out of an application to the ordinary affairs of life of the little tricks and ‘wrinkles' that we learn while solving recreation problems. The joiner, in the illustration, wants to cut the piece of wood into as few pieces as possible to form a square table-top, without any waste of material. How should he go to work? How many pieces would you require?
problems.promptZh: 我经常有这样的场合 评论谜题的实际效用，它源于我们在解决娱乐问题时学到的小技巧和“皱纹”在日常生活中的应用。如图所示，木工希望将木头切割成尽可能少的碎片，以形成方形桌面，而不浪费任何材料。他该怎么去上班？您需要多少件？
problems.explanation: Nothing could be easier than the solution of this puzzle—when you know how to do it. And yet it is apt to perplex the novice a good deal if he wants to do it in the fewest possible pieces—three. All you have to do is to find the point A, midway between B and C, and then cut from A to D and from A to E. The three pieces then form a square in the manner shown. Of course, the proportions of the original figure must be correct; thus the triangle BEF is just a quarter of the square BCDF. Draw lines from B to D and from C to F and this will be clear.
problems.explanationEn: Nothing could be easier than the solution of this puzzle—when you know how to do it. And yet it is apt to perplex the novice a good deal if he wants to do it in the fewest possible pieces—three. All you have to do is to find the point A, midway between B and C, and then cut from A to D and from A to E. The three pieces then form a square in the manner shown. Of course, the proportions of the original figure must be correct; thus the triangle BEF is just a quarter of the square BCDF. Draw lines from B to D and from C to F and this will be clear.
problems.explanationZh: 当您知道如何做时，没有什么比解决这个难题更容易的了。然而，如果新手想用尽可能少的部分（三块）来完成它，那么他很容易会感到困惑。您所要做的就是找到 B 和 C 中间的 A 点，然后从 A 到 D、从 A 到 E 进行切割。然后，这三个部分按所示方式形成一个正方形。当然，原图的比例一定要正确；因此三角形 BEF 只是正方形 BCDF 的四分之一。从 B 到 D、从 C 到 F 画一条线，这样就很清楚了。

problems.promptEn: A joiner had two pieces of wood of the shapes and relative proportions shown in the diagram. He wished to cut them into as few pieces as possible so that they could be fitted together, without waste, to form a perfectly square table- top. How should he have done it? There is no necessity to give measurements, for if the smaller piece (which is half a square) be made a little too large or a little too small it will not affect the method of solution.
problems.promptZh: 木匠有两块形状和相关的木头 比例如图所示。他希望将它们切成尽可能少的碎片，这样它们就可以毫无浪费地拼凑在一起，形成一个完美的方形桌面。他应该怎样做呢？没有必要进行测量，因为如果较小的一块（半个正方形）做得太大或太小，都不会影响解决方法。
problems.explanation: The task is to form a perfect square from a square combined with a right-angled isosceles triangle. The exact relative sizes of the square and triangle do not matter, as long as the triangle is not larger in area than the square. In the diagram, let the original square be ACLF and the triangular half-square be CED. First take half the length of the long side CD and mark that length as AB. Then place the triangle against the square as shown and make just two cuts, BF and BE. Move pieces G, H, and M to the positions indicated in the diagram, and the five pieces form the square BEKF. No piece needs to be turned over.
problems.explanationEn: The task is to form a perfect square from a square combined with a right-angled isosceles triangle. The exact relative sizes of the square and triangle do not matter, as long as the triangle is not larger in area than the square. In the diagram, let the original square be ACLF and the triangular half-square be CED. First take half the length of the long side CD and mark that length as AB. Then place the triangle against the square as shown and make just two cuts, BF and BE. Move pieces G, H, and M to the positions indicated in the diagram, and the five pieces form the square BEKF. No piece needs to be turned over.
problems.explanationZh: 本题的关键，是把一个正方形和一个直角等腰三角形（也就是半个正方形）拼成一个完整正方形。只要三角形面积不大于原正方形，二者的精确比例并不重要。图中设原正方形为 ACLF，阴影三角形为 CED。先取长边 CD 的一半，在 AB 上量出同样长度；然后按图把三角形靠在正方形旁，只需作两刀：BF 和 BE。把 G、H、M 三块移到图中所示的新位置，五块便组成正方形 BEKF，而且不需要翻转任何一块。

problems.promptEn: Can you cut this chain out of a piece of cardboard without any join whatever? Every link is solid; without its having been split and afterwards joined at any place. It is an interesting old puzzle that I learnt as a child, but I have no knowledge as to its inventor.
problems.promptZh: 你能从一块纸板上剪下这条链条吗？ 任何加入什么？每个环节都牢固；它没有被分割然后在任何地方连接。这是我小时候学过的一个有趣的古老谜题，但我不知道它的发明者。
problems.explanation: The reader will probably feel rewarded for any care and patience that he may bestow on cutting out the cardboard chain. We will suppose that he has a piece of cardboard measuring 8 in. by 2½ in., though the dimensions are of no importance. Yet if you want a long chain you must, of course, take a long strip of cardboard. First rule pencil lines B B and C C, half an inch from the edges, and also the short perpendicular lines half an inch apart. (See next page.) Rule lines on the other side in just the same way, and in order that they shall coincide it is well to prick through the card with a needle the points where the short lines end. Now take your penknife and split the card from A A down to B B, and from D D up to C C. Then cut right through the card along all the short perpendicular lines, and half through the card along the short portions of B B and C C that are not dotted. Next turn the card over and cut half through along the short lines on B B and C C at the places that are immediately beneath the dotted lines on the upper side. With a little careful separation of the parts with the penknife, the cardboard may now be divided into two interlacing ladder- like portions, as shown in Fig. 2; and if you cut away all the shaded parts you will get the chain, cut solidly out of the cardboard, without any join, as shown in the illustrations on page 40. It is an interesting variant of the puzzle to cut out two keys on a ring—in the same manner without join.
problems.explanationEn: The reader will probably feel rewarded for any care and patience that he may bestow on cutting out the cardboard chain. We will suppose that he has a piece of cardboard measuring 8 in. by 2½ in., though the dimensions are of no importance. Yet if you want a long chain you must, of course, take a long strip of cardboard. First rule pencil lines B B and C C, half an inch from the edges, and also the short perpendicular lines half an inch apart. (See next page.) Rule lines on the other side in just the same way, and in order that they shall coincide it is well to prick through the card with a needle the points where the short lines end. Now take your penknife and split the card from A A down to B B, and from D D up to C C. Then cut right through the card along all the short perpendicular lines, and half through the card along the short portions of B B and C C that are not dotted. Next turn the card over and cut half through along the short lines on B B and C C at the places that are immediately beneath the dotted lines on the upper side. With a little careful separation of the parts with the penknife, the cardboard may now be divided into two interlacing ladder- like portions, as shown in Fig. 2; and if you cut away all the shaded parts you will get the chain, cut solidly out of the cardboard, without any join, as shown in the illustrations on page 40. It is an interesting variant of the puzzle to cut out two keys on a ring—in the same manner without join.
problems.explanationZh: 读者可能会因为在剪下纸板链时所付出的细心和耐心而感到得到回报。我们假设他有一块 8 英寸 x 2.5 英寸的纸板，尽管尺寸并不重要。然而，如果你想要一条长链条，你当然必须拿一条长纸板。首先划出距边缘半英寸的铅笔线 B B 和 C C，以及相距半英寸的短垂直线。 （参见下页。）以同样的方式在另一侧划线，为了使它们重合，最好用针在卡片上刺穿短线结束的点。现在拿起小刀，将卡片从 A A 向下切到 B B，从 D D 向上切到 C C。然后沿着所有短垂直线将卡片切开，沿着 B B 和 C C 的未虚线的短部分将卡片切开一半。接下来将卡片翻过来，沿着 B B 和 C C 上的短线在上侧虚线正下方的位置切一半。用小刀小心地将各个部分分开，纸板现在可以分成两个交错的梯状部分，如图 2 所示；如果你剪掉所有阴影部分，你就会得到链条，从纸板上牢固地切下来，没有任何连接，如第 40 页的插图所示。这是拼图的一个有趣的变体，在环上剪出两把钥匙——以同样的方式没有连接。

problems.promptEn: A wizard placed ten cats inside a magic circle as shown in our illustration, and hypnotized them so that they should remain stationary during his pleasure. He then proposed to draw three circles inside the large one, so that no cat could approach another cat without crossing a magic circle. Try to draw the three circles so that every cat has its own enclosure and cannot reach another cat without crossing a line.
problems.promptZh: 一个巫师把十只猫放进去 如我们插图所示的一个魔法阵，并对他们进行催眠，以便他们在他高兴的时候保持静止。然后他提议在大圆圈内画三个圆圈，这样任何猫都不能在不穿过魔法阵的情况下接近另一只猫。尝试画三个圆圈，让每只猫都有自己的围栏，并且在不越线的情况下无法到达另一只猫。
problems.explanation: The illustration requires no explanation. It shows clearly how the three circles may be drawn so that every cat has a separate enclosure, and cannot approach another cat without crossing a line.
problems.explanationEn: The illustration requires no explanation. It shows clearly how the three circles may be drawn so that every cat has a separate enclosure, and cannot approach another cat without crossing a line.
problems.explanationZh: 插图不需要解释。它清楚地显示了如何绘制三个圆圈，以便每只猫都有一个单独的围栏，并且在不越界的情况下无法接近另一只猫。

problems.promptEn: Many pastimes of great antiquity, such as chess, have so developed and changed down the centuries that their original inventors would scarcely recognize them. This is not the case with Tangrams, a recreation that appears to be at least four thousand years old, that has apparently never been dormant, and that has not been altered or ‘improved upon' since the legendary Tan first cut out the seven pieces shown in Diagram I. If you mark the point B, midway between A and C, on one side of a square of any size, and D, midway between C and E, on an adjoining side, the direction of the cuts is too obvious to need further explanation. Every design in this article is built up from the seven pieces of blackened cardboard. It will at once be understood that the possible combinations are infinite. The late Mr. Sam Loyd, of New York, who published a small book of very ingenious designs, possessed the manuscripts of the late Mr. Challenor, who made a long and close study of Tangrams. This gentleman, it is said, records that there were originally seven books of Tangrams, compiled in China two thousand years before the Christian era. These books are so rare that, after forty years' residence in the country, he only succeeded in seeing perfect copies of the first and seventh volumes with fragments of the second. Portions of one of the books, printed in gold leaf upon parchment, were found in Peking by an English soldier and sold for three hundred pounds. A few years ago a little book came into my possession, from the library of the late Lewis Carroll, entitled The Fashionable Chinese Puzzle. It contains three hundred and twenty-three Tangram designs, mostly nondescript geometrical figures, to be constructed from the seven pieces. It was ‘Published by J. and E. Wallis, 42 Skinner Street, and J. Wallis, Jun., Marine Library, Sidmouth' (South Devon). There is no date, but the following note fixes the time of publication pretty closely: ‘This ingenious contrivance has for some time past been the favourite amusement of the ex-Emperor Napoleon, who, being now in a debilitated state and living very retired, passes many hours a day in thus exercising his patience and ingenuity.' The reader will find, as did the great exile, that much amusement, not wholly uninstructive, may be derived from forming the designs of others. He will find many of the illustrations to this article quite easy to build up, and some rather difficult. Every picture may thus be regarded as a puzzle. But it is another pastime altogether to create new and original designs of a pictorial character, and it is surprising what extraordinary scope the Tangrams afford for producing pictures of real life—angular and often grotesque, it is true, but full of character. I give an example of a recumbent figure (2) that is particularly graceful, and only needs some slight reduction of its angularities to produce an entirely satisfactory outline. As I have referred to the author of Alice in Wonderland, I give also my designs of the March Hare (3) and the Hatter (4). I also give an attempt at Napoleon (5), and a very excellent Red Indian with his Squaw by Mr. Loyd (6 and 7). A large number of other designs will be found in an article by me in The Strand Magazine for November, 1908. On the appearance of this magazine article, the late Sir James Murray, the eminent philologist, tried, with that amazing industry that characterized all his work, to trace the word ‘tangram' to its source. At length he wrote as follows:—‘One of my sons is a professor in the Anglo- Chinese college at Tientsin. Through him, his colleagues, and his students, I was able to make inquiries as to the alleged Tan among Chinese scholars. Our Chinese professor here (Oxford) also took an interest in the matter and obtained information from the secretary of the Chinese Legation in London, who is a very eminent representative of the Chinese literati.' ‘The result has been to show that the man Tan, the god Tan, and the ‘Book of Tan' are entirely unknown to Chinese literature, history, or tradition. By most of the learned men the name, or allegation of the existence, of these had never been heard of. The puzzle is, of course, well known. It is called in Chinese ch'i ch'iao t'u; literally, ‘seven-ingenious- plan' or ‘ingenious-puzzle figure of seven pieces.' No name approaching ‘tangram,' or even ‘tan,' occurs in Chinese, and the only suggestions for the latter were the Chinese t'an, ‘to extend'; or t'ang, Cantonese dialect for ‘Chinese.' It was suggested that probably some American or Englishman who knew a little Chinese or Cantonese, wanting a name for the puzzle, might concoct one out of one of these words and the European ending ‘gram.' I should say the name ‘tangram' was probably invented by an American some little time before 1864 and after 1847, but I cannot find it in print before the 1864 edition of Webster. I have therefore had to deal very shortly with the word in the dictionary, telling what it is applied to and what conjectures or guesses have been made at the name, and giving a few quotations, one from your own article, which has enabled me to make more of the subject than I could otherwise have done.' Several correspondents have informed me that they possess, or had possessed, specimens of the old Chinese books. An American gentleman writes to me as follows:—‘I have in my possession a book made of tissue paper, printed in black (with a Chinese inscription on the front page), containing over three hundred designs, which belongs to the box of ‘tangrams,' which I also own. The blocks are seven in number, made of mother-of-pearl, highly polished and finely engraved on either side. These are contained in a rosewood box 2 in. square. My great uncle was one of the first missionaries to visit China. This box and book, along with quite a collection of other relics, were sent to my grandfather and descended to myself.' My correspondent kindly supplied me with rubbings of the Tangrams, from which it is clear that they are cut in the exact proportions that I have indicated. I reproduce the Chinese inscription (8) for this reason. By using several sets of Tangrams at the same time we may construct more ambitious pictures. I was advised by a friend not to send my picture, ‘A Game of Billiards' (9), to the Academy. He assured me that it would not be accepted because the ‘judges are so hide-bound by convention.' Perhaps he was right, and it will be more appreciated by Post-impressionists and Cubists. The players are considering a very delicate stroke at the top of the table. Of course, the two men, the table, and the clock are formed from four sets of Tangrams. My second picture is named ‘The Orchestra' (10), and it was designed for the decoration of a large hall of music. Here we have the conductor, the pianist, the fat little cornet-player, the left-handed player of the double-bass, whose attitude is life-like, though he does stand at an unusual distance from his instrument, and the drummer-boy, with his imposing music-stand. The dog at the back of the pianoforte is not howling: he is an appreciative listener. One remarkable thing about these Tangram pictures is that they suggest to the imagination such a lot that is not really there. Who, for example, can look for a few minutes at Lady Belinda (11) and the Dutch girl (12) without soon feeling the haughty expression in the one case and the arch look in the other? Then look again at the stork (13), and see how it is suggested to the mind that the leg is actually much more slender than any one of the pieces employed. It is really an optical illusion. Again, notice in the case of the yacht (14) how, by leaving that little angular point at the top, a complete mast is suggested. If you place your Tangrams together on white paper so that they do not quite touch one another, in some cases the effect is improved by the white lines; in other cases it is almost destroyed. Finally, I give an example from the many curious paradoxes that one happens upon in manipulating Tangrams. I show designs of two dignified individuals (15 and 16) who appear to be exactly alike, except for the fact that one has a foot and the other has not. Now, both of these figures are made from the same seven Tangrams. Where does the second man get his foot from? ‘Of shreds and patches.' —Hamlet, iii. 4.
problems.promptZh: 许多古代的消遣活动，例如国际象棋，都有这样的意义 几个世纪以来，它们不断发展和变化，以至于它们的最初发明者几乎认不出它们了。七巧板的情况并非如此，它是一种似乎至少有四千年历史的游戏，而且显然从未休眠过，，自从传说中的谭第一次剪出图一所示的七块以来，这一问题就没有被改变或“改进”。如果你在任何大小的正方形的一侧标记A和C中间的点B，在相邻一侧标记C和E之间的D点，切割的方向太明显了，不需要进一步解释。本文中的每个设计都是由七块黑色纸板组成的。立刻就会明白，可能的组合是无限的。纽约已故的萨姆·洛伊德先生出版了一本设计非常巧妙的小书，他拥有已故的查勒诺先生的手稿，查勒诺先生对七巧板进行了长期而仔细的研究。据说，这位先生记载，早在公元两千多年前，中国就有七本七巧板。这些书实在是太稀有了，他在国内居住了四十年，也只看到了第一卷和第七卷的完美副本，以及第二卷的残片。其中一本书的部分内容用金箔印在羊皮纸上，被一名英国士兵在北京发现，并以三百英镑的价格出售。几年前，我从已故刘易斯·卡罗尔的图书馆里得到了一本小书，书名叫《时尚的中国谜题》。它包含三百二十三个七巧板设计，大部分是不起眼的几何图形，由七块拼图组成。它的出版者是“J. 和 E. Wallis，42 Skinner Street，J. Wallis, Jun.，海洋图书馆，西德茅斯”（南德文郡）。没有日期，但下面的注释非常接近地确定了出版时间：“这个巧妙的发明在过去一段时间里一直是最受欢迎的 前皇帝拿破仑感到很有趣，他现在身体虚弱，过着退休的生活，每天花很多时间来锻炼他的耐心和聪明才智。读者会发现，正如伟大的流亡者所做的那样，许多乐趣并非完全没有启发性，可以从形成他人的设计中获得。他会发现本文的许多插图很容易构建，而有些则相当困难。因此，每张图片都可以被视为一个谜题。但创造新的、原创的图形角色设计完全是另一种消遣，而且令人惊讶的是七巧板在制作现实生活的图片方面所提供的非凡范围——确实是有棱角的，而且常常是怪诞的，但充满了个性。我举了一个卧姿 (2) 的例子，它特别优美，只需要稍微减少其角度就可以产生完全令人满意的轮廓。正如我提到了《爱丽丝梦游仙境》的作者，我也给出了我对三月兔 (3) 和帽匠 (4) 的设计。我还尝试了拿破仑（5），以及劳埃德先生（6 和 7）的非常出色的红印第安人和他的 Squaw。大量其他设​​计可以在我于 1908 年 11 月发表在《Strand》杂志上的一篇文章中找到。在这篇杂志文章出现时，已故的著名语言学家詹姆斯·默里爵士试图以他所有作品中所特有的令人惊叹的勤奋来追踪“七巧板”一词的来源。最后他写道： 我儿子的 是天津英华学院的教授。通过他、他的同事和他的学生，我得以在中国学者中查询到所谓的谭。我们这里（牛津）的华人教授也对此事感兴趣，并从伦敦中国使馆秘书处获得了信息，他是中国文人的非常杰出的代表。结果表明，谭人、谭神和《谭书》对于中国文学、历史或传统来说是完全未知的。对于大多数有学问的人来说，这些的名字或存在的说法从未听说过。当然，这个谜题是众所周知的。它在中文中被称为“ch'i ch'iao t'u”；字面意思是“七个巧妙的计划”或“七个巧妙的拼图”。汉语中没有出现类似“七巧板”甚至“tan”的名称，对后者的唯一建议是汉语“t'an”，“延伸”；或 t'ang，粤语方言，意为“中国人”。有人建议，也许一些懂一点中文或粤语的美国人或英国人想要给这个谜题起一个名字，可能会用这些单词中的一个和欧洲词尾“gram”编造出一个名字。我应该说“七巧板”这个名字可能是由美国人在 1864 年之前和 1847 年之后不久发明的，但我在 1864 年版《韦氏词典》之前找不到它的印刷品。因此，我不得不很快地处理字典中的这个词，告诉它适用于什么以及对这个名字做出了哪些猜想或猜测，并给出了一些引文，其中之一来自你自己的文章，这使我能够比我本来可以做的更多地讨论这个主题。 几位记者告诉我，他们拥有或曾经拥有中国古籍的样本。一位美国绅士给我写信说：“我有一本薄纸做的书，黑字印刷（首页有中文铭文），有三百多个图案，属于‘七巧板’盒子，我也有。”这些石块共有七块，由珍珠母贝制成，经过高度抛光，两侧都有精美雕刻。它们装在一个 2 英寸见方的红木盒子里。我的叔祖父是最早访问中国的传教士之一。这个盒子和书，以及相当多的其他文物，被送到了我的祖父那里，并传给了我自己。我的通讯员好心地向我提供了七巧板的拓片，从中可以清楚地看出，它们是按照我指定的确切比例切割的。为此，我复制了中文铭文（8）。通过同时使用多组七巧板，我们可以构建更雄心勃勃的图片。一位朋友建议我 不要将我的照片“台球游戏”(9) 发送给学院。他向我保证，它不会被接受，因为“法官们太墨守成规了。”也许他是对的，后印象派和立体派会更欣赏它。球员们正在考虑在积分榜上进行非常微妙的击球。当然，两个人、桌子和时钟都是由四组七巧板组成的。我的第二张照片名为“管弦乐队”（10），它是为装饰大型音乐厅而设计的。这里有指挥家、钢琴家、胖乎乎的小号吹奏者、左手低音提琴手，他的态度栩栩如生，尽管他与乐器的距离确实不寻常，还有鼓手男孩，他的乐谱架很壮观。钢琴后面的狗并没有嚎叫：它是一位懂得欣赏的倾听者。这些七巧板图片的一个显着之处在于，它们给人们的想象力带来了很多实际上并不存在的东西。例如，谁能花几分钟观察贝琳达女士（11 岁）和荷兰女孩（12 岁），而不会很快感受到其中一个案例中傲慢的表情和另一个案例中拱形的外观？然后 再看看鹳 (13)，看看它如何向大脑暗示，它的腿实际上比所使用的任何一个部件都要细长得多。这确实是一种视错觉。再次注意，在游艇 (14) 的情况下，如何通过在顶部留下那个小角点来建议使用完整的桅杆。如果您将七巧板放在白纸上，使它们彼此不完全接触，在某些情况下，白线可以改善效果；在其他情况下，它几乎被摧毁。最后，我举了一个在操纵七巧板时遇到的许多奇怪悖论的例子。我展示了两个有尊严的人（15 岁和 16 岁）的设计，他们看起来一模一样，除了一个有脚而另一个没有脚。现在，这两个人物都是由相同的七个七巧板组成的。第二个人的脚从哪里来？
problems.explanation: The diagrams show how the two figures are constructed, each from the same seven Tangram pieces. In both figures the head, hat, and arm are exactly alike, and the width at the base of the body is the same. The difference is that the body of the first figure contains four pieces while the body of the second contains only three. The first body is larger by the narrow strip indicated by the dotted line between A and B; that strip is exactly equal in area to the foot in the other design.
problems.explanationEn: The diagrams show how the two figures are constructed, each from the same seven Tangram pieces. In both figures the head, hat, and arm are exactly alike, and the width at the base of the body is the same. The difference is that the body of the first figure contains four pieces while the body of the second contains only three. The first body is larger by the narrow strip indicated by the dotted line between A and B; that strip is exactly equal in area to the foot in the other design.
problems.explanationZh: 图示说明了两个图形如何都由同一套七巧板拼成。两图的头、帽子和手臂完全相同，身体底部的宽度也相同。差别在于，第一个图形的身体用了四块，而第二个图形的身体只用了三块。第一个身体多出的面积，正是 A 与 B 之间虚线所标出的那条窄条；这条窄条的面积恰好等于另一个图形中脚的面积。

problems.promptEn: The above represents a square of brocade. A lady wishes to cut it in four pieces so that two pieces will form one perfectly square cushion top, and the remaining two pieces another square cushion top. How is she to do it? Of course, she can only cut along the lines that divide the twenty-five squares, and the pattern must ‘match' properly without any irregularity whatever in the design of the material. There is only one way of doing it. Can you find it?
problems.promptZh: 以上代表 一块正方形的锦缎。一位女士希望将其切成四块，这样两块将形成一个完美的方形垫顶，其余两块将形成另一个方形垫顶。她该怎么办呢？当然，她只能沿着二十五个方格的分割线进行切割，并且图案必须“匹配”得当，不能有任何材料设计上的不规则。只有一种方法可以做到这一点。你能找到吗？
problems.explanation: The two pieces of brocade marked A will fit together and form one perfect square cushion top, and the two pieces marked B will form the other.
problems.explanationEn: The two pieces of brocade marked A will fit together and form one perfect square cushion top, and the two pieces marked B will form the other.
problems.explanationZh: 标记为 A 的两块锦缎将组合在一起，形成一个完美的方形垫顶，标记为 B 的两块锦缎将形成另一块。

problems.titleZh: 40. 史迈利夫人的圣诞礼物。
problems.promptEn: Mrs. Smiley's expression of pleasure was sincere when her six granddaughters sent to her, as a Christmas present, a very pretty patchwork quilt, which they had made with their own hands. It was constructed of square pieces of silk material, all of one size, and as they made a large quilt with fourteen of these little squares on each side, it is obvious that just 196 pieces had been stitched into it. Now, the six granddaughters each contributed a part of the work in the form of a perfect square (all six portions being different in size), but in order to join them up to form the square quilt it was necessary that the work of one girl should be unpicked into three separate pieces. Can you show how the joins might have been made? Of course, no portion can be turned over.
problems.promptZh: 史迈利夫人的高兴表情是真诚的 六个孙女送给她一床非常漂亮的拼布被子作为圣诞礼物，这是她们亲手缝制的。它是用正方形的丝绸材料制成的，尺寸都是一样的，当他们用每边十四个这样的小正方形制作一床大被子时，很明显只缝了 196 块。现在，六个孙女各自以完美正方形的形式贡献了一部分作品（六部分大小不同），但为了加入 为了拼成方形被子，必须将一个女孩的作品拆成三块。您能展示如何进行连接吗？当然，任何部分都不能翻过来。
problems.explanation: First find six different square numbers whose sum is 196. For example, 1 + 4 + 25 + 36 + 49 + 81 = 196; another choice is 1 + 4 + 9 + 25 + 36 + 121 = 196; and a third is 1 + 9 + 16 + 25 + 64 + 81 = 196. The rest is a dissection problem: the diagrams show solutions for the first two sets. In each case the three pieces marked A, together with the other indicated pieces, can be rearranged to form the 14 by 14 square. The assembly can be varied slightly, and the reader may try to construct a solution for the third set.
problems.explanationEn: First find six different square numbers whose sum is 196. For example, 1 + 4 + 25 + 36 + 49 + 81 = 196; another choice is 1 + 4 + 9 + 25 + 36 + 121 = 196; and a third is 1 + 9 + 16 + 25 + 64 + 81 = 196. The rest is a dissection problem: the diagrams show solutions for the first two sets. In each case the three pieces marked A, together with the other indicated pieces, can be rearranged to form the 14 by 14 square. The assembly can be varied slightly, and the reader may try to construct a solution for the third set.
problems.explanationZh: 第一步是找出六个互不相同的平方数，使它们的和为 196。例如 1 + 4 + 25 + 36 + 49 + 81 = 196；也可以取 1 + 4 + 9 + 25 + 36 + 121 = 196；还可以取 1 + 9 + 16 + 25 + 64 + 81 = 196。剩下就是剪拼问题：图中给出了前两组平方数的拼法。每种情况下，标为 A 的三块以及相应的其他部分都能重新拼成 14 x 14 的正方形。拼法可以略有变化，读者也可以尝试为第三组平方数构造拼法。

problems.titleZh: 41. 另一个拼凑而成的拼图。
problems.promptEn: A lady was presented, by two of her girl friends, with the pretty pieces of silk patchwork shown in our illustration. It will be seen that both pieces are made up of squares all of the same size—one 12 \times 12 and the other 5 \times 5. She proposes to join them together and make one square patchwork quilt, 13 \times 13, but, of course, she will not cut any of the material—merely cut the stitches where necessary and join together again. What perplexes her is this. A friend assures her that there need be no more than four pieces in all to join up for the new quilt. Could you show her how this little needlework puzzle is to be solved in so few pieces?
problems.promptZh: 一位女士由她的两个女朋友与漂亮的人一起介绍 我们插图中显示的丝绸拼凑而成的碎片。这将是 看到这两块都是由相同尺寸的正方形组成的——一个是 12 × 12，另一个是 5 × 5。她建议将它们连接在一起，制作一个方形拼布被子，13 × 13，但是，当然，她不会切割任何材料——只是在必要的地方剪掉针脚，然后再次连接在一起。让她困惑的是这个。一位朋友向她保证，新被子的拼接数量不会超过四块。你能告诉她如何用这么几块拼图来解决这个小针线难题吗？
problems.explanation: The lady need only unpick the stitches along the dark lines in the larger portion of patchwork, when the four pieces will fit together and form a square, as shown in our illustration.
problems.explanationEn: The lady need only unpick the stitches along the dark lines in the larger portion of patchwork, when the four pieces will fit together and form a square, as shown in our illustration.
problems.explanationZh: 当四块拼布拼凑在一起并形成一个正方形时，女士只需沿着拼布的较大部分中的暗线解开缝线，如图所示。

problems.promptEn: The diagram herewith represents two separate pieces of linoleum. The chequered pattern is not repeated at the back, so that the pieces cannot be turned over. The puzzle is to cut the two squares into four pieces so that they shall fit together and form one perfect square 10 \times 10, so that the pattern shall properly match, and so that the larger piece shall have as small a portion as possible cut from it. ‘So various are the tastes of men.' MARK AKENSIDE.
problems.promptZh: 该图代表了两个独立的部分 油布。背面不重复格子图案，因此无法翻转。拼图是将两个正方形切成四块，使它们能够组合在一起并形成一个完美的正方形 10 × 10，这样图案就可以正确匹配，并且从较大的一块中切掉尽可能小的部分。 “男人的品味多种多样。”马克·阿肯赛德。
problems.explanation: There is only one solution that will enable us to retain the larger of the two pieces with as little as possible cut from it. Fig. 1 in the following diagram shows how the smaller piece is to be cut, and Fig. 2 how we should dissect the larger piece, while in Fig. 3 we have the new square 10 \times 10 formed by the four pieces with all the chequers properly matched. It will be seen that the piece D contains fifty-two chequers, and this is the largest
problems.explanationEn: There is only one solution that will enable us to retain the larger of the two pieces with as little as possible cut from it. Fig. 1 in the following diagram shows how the smaller piece is to be cut, and Fig. 2 how we should dissect the larger piece, while in Fig. 3 we have the new square 10 \times 10 formed by the four pieces with all the chequers properly matched. It will be seen that the piece D contains fifty-two chequers, and this is the largest
problems.explanationZh: 使我们能够保留两块中较大的一块，并尽可能少地对其进行切割。下图中的图 1 显示了如何切割较小的棋子，图 2 显示了如何解剖较大的棋子，而在图 3 中，我们有由四块棋子形成的新正方形 10 × 10，所有棋子都正确匹配。可以看出，D 棋子有五十二个棋子，这是最大的棋子。

problems.promptEn: A man possessed a square-shaped estate. He bequeathed to his widow the quarter of it that is shaded off. The remainder was to be divided equitably amongst his four sons, so that each should receive land of exactly the same area and exactly similar in shape. We are shown how this was done. In the centre of the estate was a well, indicated by the dark spot, and Benjamin, Charles, and David complained that the division was not ‘equitable,' since Alfred had access to this well, while they could not reach it without trespassing on somebody else's land. The puzzle is to show how the estate is to be apportioned so that each son shall have land of the same shape and area, and each have access to the well without going off his own land.
problems.promptZh: 一个人拥有一处方形庄园。他遗赠给 他的遗孀得到了被遮蔽的四分之一。剩余的土地将平均分配给他的四个儿子，以便每个儿子都获得面积完全相同、形状完全相同的土地。我们看到这是如何完成的。庄园的中心有一口井，如黑点所示，本杰明、查尔斯和大卫抱怨分配不“公平”，因为阿尔弗雷德可以使用这口井，而他们则无法在不侵犯别人土地的情况下到达它。该谜题是为了说明如何分配遗产，使每个儿子都拥有相同形状和面积的土地，并且每个儿子都可以在不离开自己的土地的情况下使用井。
problems.explanation: The diagram shows the most equitable division of the land possible, “so that each son shall receive land of exactly the same area and exactly similar in shape,” and so that each shall have access to the well in the centre without trespass on another's land. The conditions do not require that each son's land shall be in one piece, but it is necessary that the two portions assigned to an individual should be kept apart, or two adjoining portions might be held to be one piece, in which case the condition as to shape would have to be broken. At present there is only one shape for each piece of land—half a square divided diagonally. And A, B, C, and D can each reach their land from the outside, and have each equal access to the well in the centre.
problems.explanationEn: The diagram shows the most equitable division of the land possible, “so that each son shall receive land of exactly the same area and exactly similar in shape,” and so that each shall have access to the well in the centre without trespass on another's land. The conditions do not require that each son's land shall be in one piece, but it is necessary that the two portions assigned to an individual should be kept apart, or two adjoining portions might be held to be one piece, in which case the condition as to shape would have to be broken. At present there is only one shape for each piece of land—half a square divided diagonally. And A, B, C, and D can each reach their land from the outside, and have each equal access to the well in the centre.
problems.explanationZh: 土地的划分成为可能，“以便每个儿子都获得面积完全相同、形状完全相同的土地”，并且每个儿子都可以使用中央的井而不会侵犯他人的土地。条件并不要求每个儿子的土地必须是一块，但必须将分配给个人的两部分土地分开，或者可以将相邻的两部分土地视为一块，在这种情况下，形状的条件就必须被打破。目前，每块土地只有一种形状——对角分割的半个正方形。 A、B、C 和 D 都可以从外部到达自己的土地，并且可以平等地进入中心的井。

problems.promptEn: As I sat in a railway carriage I noticed at the other end of the compartment a worthy squire, whom I knew by sight, engaged in conversation with another passenger, who was evidently a friend of his. ‘How far have you to drive to your place from the railway station?' asked the stranger. ‘Well,' replied the squire, ‘if I get out at Appleford, it is just the same distance as if I go to Bridgefield, another fifteen miles farther on; and if I changed at Appleford and went thirteen miles from there to Carterton, it would still be the same distance. You see, I am equidistant from the three stations, so I get a good choice of trains.' Now I happened to know that Bridgefield is just fourteen miles from Carterton, so I amused myself in working out the exact distance that the squire had to drive home whichever station he got out at. What was the distance?
problems.promptZh: 当我坐在火车车厢里时，我注意到火车的另一端 车厢里有一位可敬的乡绅，我一眼就认识他，正在和另一位乘客交谈，那人显然是他的朋友。 “从火车站到你住的地方要多远？”陌生人问道。 “好吧，”乡绅回答道，“如果我从阿普尔福德出去，那只是 与我去布里奇菲尔德的距离相同，但还要再远 15 英里；如果我在阿普尔福德转车，从那里走十三英里到卡特顿，距离仍然是一样的。你看，我离这三个车站的距离是等距离的，所以我有很好的火车选择。”现在我碰巧知道布里奇菲尔德距离卡特顿只有十四英里，所以我自娱自乐地计算出乡绅无论在哪个车站下车，必须开车回家的确切距离。距离是多少？
problems.explanation: The three stations form a triangle, with sides 13, 14, and 15 miles. Make the 14 side the base; then the height of the triangle is 12 and the area 84. Multiply the three sides together and divide by four times the area. The result is eight miles and one-eighth, the distance required.
problems.explanationEn: The three stations form a triangle, with sides 13, 14, and 15 miles. Make the 14 side the base; then the height of the triangle is 12 and the area 84. Multiply the three sides together and divide by four times the area. The result is eight miles and one-eighth, the distance required.
problems.explanationZh: 这三个车站形成一个三角形，边长分别为 13、14 和 15 英里。将14边作为底面；则三角形的高为 12，面积为 84。将三边相乘，然后除以面积的四倍。结果是八英里又八分之一，即所需的距离。

problems.promptEn: If you hold the page horizontally and give it a quick rotary motion while looking at the centre of the spiral, it will appear to revolve. Perhaps a good many readers are acquainted with this little optical illusion. But the puzzle is to show how I was able to draw this spiral with so much exactitude without using anything but a pair of compasses and the sheet of paper on which the diagram was made. How would you proceed in such circumstances?
problems.promptZh: 如果您水平握住页面并快速查看 旋转运动，同时看螺旋的中心，它会出现旋转。也许很多读者都熟悉这种小小的视错觉。但难题是要展示我如何能够如此精确地绘制出这个螺旋，而无需使用任何东西，除了一对圆规和一张制作图表的纸之外。在这种情况下你会如何进行？
problems.explanation: Make a fold in the paper, as shown by the dotted line in the illustration. Then, taking any two points, as A and B, describe semicircles on the line alternately from the centres B and A, being careful to make the ends join, and the thing is done. Of course this is not a true spiral, but the puzzle was to produce the particular spiral that was shown, and that was drawn in this simple manner.
problems.explanationEn: Make a fold in the paper, as shown by the dotted line in the illustration. Then, taking any two points, as A and B, describe semicircles on the line alternately from the centres B and A, being careful to make the ends join, and the thing is done. Of course this is not a true spiral, but the puzzle was to produce the particular spiral that was shown, and that was drawn in this simple manner.
problems.explanationZh: 图中的虚线所示。然后，取任意两点，作为A和B，从B和A的中心交替地在直线上画出半圆，注意使两端连接起来，事情就完成了。当然这不是一个 真正的螺旋，但难题是产生所显示的特定螺旋，并以这种简单的方式绘制。

problems.promptEn: I was on a visit to one of the large towns of Yorkshire. While walking to the railway station on the day of my departure a man thrust a hand-bill upon me, and I took this into the railway carriage and read it at my leisure. It informed me that three Yorkshire neighbouring estates were to be offered for sale. Each estate was square in shape, and they joined one another at their corners, just as shown in the diagram. Estate A contains exactly 370 acres, B contains 116 acres, and C 74 acres. Now, the little triangular bit of land enclosed by the three square estates was not offered for sale, and, for no reason in particular, I became curious as to the area of that piece. How many acres did it contain?
problems.promptZh: 我正在参观约克郡的一个大城镇。尽管 出发那天，在去火车站的路上，一个人塞给我一张传单，我把它带上火车车厢，闲暇时读起来。它告诉我，约克郡邻近的三个庄园将被出售。每个庄园都是方形的，它们在拐角处相互连接，如图所示。财产 A 正好包含 370 英亩，B 包含 116 英亩，C 包含 74 英亩。现在，三个方形庄园围成的那块三角形的小土地没有出售，而且，没有什么特别的原因，我对那块土地的面积感到好奇。它包含多少英亩？
problems.explanation: The triangular piece of land that was not for sale contains exactly eleven acres. Of course it is not difficult to find the answer if we follow the eccentric and tricky tracks of intricate trigonometry; or I might say that the application of a well- known formula reduces the problem to finding one-quarter of the square root of (4 \times 370 \times 116) - (370 + 116 - 74)²—that is a quarter of the square root of 1936, which is one-quarter of 44, or 11 acres. But all that the reader really requires to know is the Pythagorean law on which many puzzles have been built, that in any right-angled triangle the square of the hypotenuse is equal to the sum of the squares of the other two sides. I shall dispense with all “surds” and similar absurdities, notwithstanding the fact that the sides of our triangle are clearly incommensurate, since we cannot exactly extract the square roots of the three square areas. In the above diagram ABC represents our triangle. ADB is a right-angled triangle, AD measuring 9 and BD measuring 17, because the square of 9 added to the square of 17 equals 370, the known area of the square on AB. Also AEC is a right-angled triangle, and the square of 5 added to the square of 7 equals 74, the square estate on A C. Similarly, CFB is a right-angled triangle, for the square of 4 added to the square of 10 equals 116, the square estate on BC. Now, although the sides of our triangular estate are incommensurate, we have in this diagram all the exact figures that we need to discover the area with precision. The area of our triangle ADB is clearly half of 9 \times 17, or 76½ acres. The area of AEC is half of 5 \times 7, or 17½ acres; the area of CFB is half of 4 \times 10, or 20 acres; and the area of the oblong EDFC is obviously 4 \times 7, or 28 acres. Now, if we add together 17½, 20, and 28 = 65½, and deduct this sum from the area of the large triangle ADB (which we have found to be 76½ acres), what remains must clearly be the area of ABC. That is to say, the area we want must be 76½ - 65½ = 11 acres exactly.
problems.explanationEn: The triangular piece of land that was not for sale contains exactly eleven acres. Of course it is not difficult to find the answer if we follow the eccentric and tricky tracks of intricate trigonometry; or I might say that the application of a well- known formula reduces the problem to finding one-quarter of the square root of (4 \times 370 \times 116) - (370 + 116 - 74)²—that is a quarter of the square root of 1936, which is one-quarter of 44, or 11 acres. But all that the reader really requires to know is the Pythagorean law on which many puzzles have been built, that in any right-angled triangle the square of the hypotenuse is equal to the sum of the squares of the other two sides. I shall dispense with all “surds” and similar absurdities, notwithstanding the fact that the sides of our triangle are clearly incommensurate, since we cannot exactly extract the square roots of the three square areas. In the above diagram ABC represents our triangle. ADB is a right-angled triangle, AD measuring 9 and BD measuring 17, because the square of 9 added to the square of 17 equals 370, the known area of the square on AB. Also AEC is a right-angled triangle, and the square of 5 added to the square of 7 equals 74, the square estate on A C. Similarly, CFB is a right-angled triangle, for the square of 4 added to the square of 10 equals 116, the square estate on BC. Now, although the sides of our triangular estate are incommensurate, we have in this diagram all the exact figures that we need to discover the area with precision. The area of our triangle ADB is clearly half of 9 \times 17, or 76½ acres. The area of AEC is half of 5 \times 7, or 17½ acres; the area of CFB is half of 4 \times 10, or 20 acres; and the area of the oblong EDFC is obviously 4 \times 7, or 28 acres. Now, if we add together 17½, 20, and 28 = 65½, and deduct this sum from the area of the large triangle ADB (which we have found to be 76½ acres), what remains must clearly be the area of ABC. That is to say, the area we want must be 76½ - 65½ = 11 acres exactly.
problems.explanationZh: 这块未出售的三角形土地正好有十一英亩。当然，如果我们遵循错综复杂的三角学的古怪而棘手的轨迹，就不难找到答案。或者我可能会说，应用一个众所周知的公式可以将问题简化为求 (4 × 370 × 116) - (370 + 116 - 74)² 的四分之一平方根，即 1936 年平方根的四分之一，即 44 或 11 英亩的四分之一。但读者真正需要知道的是毕达哥拉斯定律，许多谜题都建立在毕达哥拉斯定律的基础上，即在任何直角三角形中，斜边的平方等于其他两条边的平方和。尽管我们的三角形的边明显不相称，但我将放弃所有“错误”和类似的荒谬，因为我们无法精确地提取三个正方形面积的平方根。上图中 ABC 代表我们的三角形。 ADB 是一个直角三角形，AD 的尺寸为 9，BD 的尺寸为 17，因为 9 的平方与 17 的平方相加等于 370，即 AB 上正方形的已知面积。另外，AEC 是直角三角形，5 的平方与 7 的平方相加等于 74，即 A C 上的平方。同样，CFB 是直角三角形，4 的平方与 10 的平方相加等于 116，即 BC 上的平方。现在，尽管我们的三角形区域的边不相称，但我们在该图中包含了精确发现该区域所需的所有精确数字。我们的三角形 ADB 的面积显然是 9 × 17 的一半，即 76.5 英亩。 AEC 的面积为 5 × 7 的一半，即 17½ 英亩； CFB的面积为4×10的一半，即20英亩；椭圆形 EDFC 的面积显然是 4 × 7，即 28 英亩。现在，如果我们将 17½、20 和 28 加在一起 ​​= 65½，并从大三角形 ADB 的面积（我们发现其面积为 76½ 英亩）中减去这个总和，那么剩下的显然就是 ABC 的面积。也就是说，我们想要的面积必须恰好是 76.5 - 65.5 = 11 英亩。

problems.promptEn: I will now present another land problem. The demonstration of the answer that I shall give will, I think, be found both interesting and easy of comprehension. Farmer Wurzel owned the three square fields shown in the annexed plan, containing respectively 18, 20, and 26 acres. In order to get a ring- fence round his property he bought the four intervening triangular fields. The puzzle is to discover what was then the whole area of his estate.
problems.promptZh: 我现在提出另一个土地问题。示威 我认为我给出的答案会既有趣又容易理解。农民 Wurzel 拥有所附平面图所示的三块方形田地，面积分别为 18 英亩、20 英亩和 26 英亩。为了在他的财产周围建起围栏，他买下了中间的四个三角形田地。谜题是要找出当时他的庄园的整个区域。
problems.explanation: The area of the complete estate is exactly one hundred acres. To find this answer I use the following little formula, \/4ab - (a + b + c)² where a, b, c represent the three square areas, in any order. The expression gives the area of the triangle A. This will be found to be 9 acres. It can be easily proved that A, B, C, and D are all equal in area; so the answer is 26 + 20 + 18 + 9 + 9 + 9 + 9 = 100 acres. Here is the proof. If every little dotted square in the diagram represents an acre, this must be a correct plan of the estate, for the squares of 5 and 1 together equal 26; the squares of 4 and 2 equal 20; and the squares of 3 and 3 added together equal 18. Now we see at once that the area of the triangle E is 2½, F is 4½, and G is 4. These added together make 11 acres, which we deduct from the area of the rectangle, 20 acres, and we find that the field A contains exactly 9 acres. If you want to prove that B, C, and D are equal in size to A, divide them in two by a line from the middle of the longest side to the opposite angle, and you will find that the two pieces in every case, if cut out, will exactly fit together and form A. Or we can get our proof in a still easier way. The complete area of the squared diagram is 12 \times 12 = 144 acres, and the portions 1, 2, 3, 4, not included in the estate, have the respective areas of 12½, 17½, 9½, and 4½. These added together make 44, which, deducted from 144, leaves 100 as the required area of the complete estate.
problems.explanationEn: The area of the complete estate is exactly one hundred acres. To find this answer I use the following little formula, \/4ab - (a + b + c)² where a, b, c represent the three square areas, in any order. The expression gives the area of the triangle A. This will be found to be 9 acres. It can be easily proved that A, B, C, and D are all equal in area; so the answer is 26 + 20 + 18 + 9 + 9 + 9 + 9 = 100 acres. Here is the proof. If every little dotted square in the diagram represents an acre, this must be a correct plan of the estate, for the squares of 5 and 1 together equal 26; the squares of 4 and 2 equal 20; and the squares of 3 and 3 added together equal 18. Now we see at once that the area of the triangle E is 2½, F is 4½, and G is 4. These added together make 11 acres, which we deduct from the area of the rectangle, 20 acres, and we find that the field A contains exactly 9 acres. If you want to prove that B, C, and D are equal in size to A, divide them in two by a line from the middle of the longest side to the opposite angle, and you will find that the two pieces in every case, if cut out, will exactly fit together and form A. Or we can get our proof in a still easier way. The complete area of the squared diagram is 12 \times 12 = 144 acres, and the portions 1, 2, 3, 4, not included in the estate, have the respective areas of 12½, 17½, 9½, and 4½. These added together make 44, which, deducted from 144, leaves 100 as the required area of the complete estate.
problems.explanationZh: 整个庄园的面积正好一百英亩。为了找到这个答案，我使用以下小公式， \/4ab - (a + b + c)² 其中 a、b、c 代表三个正方形区域，顺序任意。该表达式给出了三角形 A 的面积。结果为 9 英亩。容易证明A、B、C、D面积相等；所以答案是 26 + 20 + 18 + 9 + 9 + 9 + 9 = 100 英亩。这是证据。如果图中的每个小虚线方块都代表一英亩，那么这一定是正确的庄园规划，因为 5 和 1 的平方加起来等于 26； 4 和 2 的平方等于 20； 3 和 3 的平方加起来等于 18。现在我们立即看到三角形 E 的面积是 2½，F 是 4½，G 是 4。这些加起来就是 11 英亩，我们减去 根据矩形的面积 20 英亩，我们发现田地 A 正好包含 9 英亩。如果你想证明B、C、D的大小与A相等，用一条从最长边的中间到对角的线将它们分成两部分，你会发现，在任何情况下，这两个部分，如果剪下来，都会完全吻合形成A。或者我们可以用更简单的方法得到我们的证明。平方图的总面积为 12 × 12 = 144 英亩，不包括在庄园内的部分 1、2、3、4 的面积分别为 12½、17½、9½ 和 4½。这些加起来就是 44，从 144 中扣除，剩下 100 作为整个庄园所需的面积。

problems.promptEn: Here is a new little puzzle with matches. It will be seen in the illustration that thirteen matches, representing a farmer's hurdles, have been so placed that they enclose six sheep-pens all of the same size. Now, one of these hurdles was stolen, and the farmer wanted still to enclose six pens of equal size with the remaining twelve. How was he to do it? All the twelve matches must be fairly used, and there must be no duplicated matches or loose ends. POINTS AND LINES PROBLEMS ‘Line upon line, line upon line; here a little and there a little.' —Isa. xxviii. 10. What are known as ‘Points and Lines' puzzles are found very interesting by many people. The most familiar example, here given, to plant nine trees so that they shall form ten straight rows with three trees in every row, is attributed to Sir Isaac Newton, but the earliest collection of such puzzles is, I believe, in a rare little book that I possess—published in 1821— Rational Amusement for Winter Evenings, by John Jackson. The author gives ten examples of ‘Trees planted in Rows.' These tree-planting puzzles have always been a matter of great perplexity. They are real ‘puzzles,' in the truest sense of the word, because nobody has yet succeeded in finding a direct and certain way of solving them. They demand the exercise of sagacity, ingenuity, and patience, and what we call ‘luck' is also sometimes of service. Perhaps some day a genius will discover the key to the whole mystery. Remember that the trees must be regarded as mere points, for if we were allowed to make our trees big enough we might easily ‘fudge' our diagrams and get in a few extra straight rows that were more apparent than real. POINTS AND LINES PROBLEMS
problems.promptZh: 这是一个新的带有火柴的小拼图。它将出现在 插图中，十三根火柴代表了一个农民的障碍，它们被放置在六个大小相同的羊圈内。现在，其中一个栏杆被偷了，农民仍然想 包含六支大小相同的笔，其余十二支笔大小相同。他该如何做呢？十二场比赛必须公平使用，不得出现重复比赛和松散的情况。 ‘线接线，线接线；这里一点，那里一点。 ——伊莎。二十八. 10. 许多人发现所谓的“点和线”谜题非常有趣。这里给出的最熟悉的例子是艾萨克·牛顿爵士（Sir Isaac Newton）种植九棵树，使其形成十排直线，每排三棵树，但我相信最早的此类谜题集是在我拥有的一本罕见的小书中，出版于 1821 年，作者是约翰·杰克逊（John Jackson），《冬季夜晚的理性娱乐》。作者举了十个“成行种植的树”的例子。这些植树难题一直是一个令人困惑的问题。从最真实的意义上来说，它们是真正的“谜题”，因为还没有人成功地找到解决它们的直接且确定的方法。他们需要智慧、独创性和耐心，而我们所说的“运气”有时也是有用的。也许有一天，一位天才会发现整个谜团的关键。请记住，树必须被视为纯粹的点，因为如果允许我们使树足够大，我们可能会很容易“捏造”我们的图表并获得一些比实际更明显的额外直行。
problems.explanation: Place the twelve matches in the manner shown in the illustration, and you will have six pens of equal size.
problems.explanationEn: Place the twelve matches in the manner shown in the illustration, and you will have six pens of equal size.
problems.explanationZh: 如图所示，您将有六支相同大小的笔。

problems.promptEn: A man had a square plantation of forty-nine trees, but, as will be seen by the omissions in the illustration, four trees were blown down and removed. He now wants to cut down all the remainder except ten trees, which are to be so left that they shall form five straight rows with four trees in every row. Which are the ten trees that he must leave?
problems.promptZh: 一个人有一个方形种植园 共有四十九棵树，但是，从图中的省略可以看出，有四棵树被吹倒并移走。现在他要把剩下的全部砍掉，除了十棵树，剩下的十棵树要形成五排直行，每排四棵树。他必须留下哪十棵树？
problems.explanation: The illustration shows the ten trees that must be left to form five rows with four trees in every row. The dots represent the positions of the trees that have been cut down.
problems.explanationEn: The illustration shows the ten trees that must be left to form five rows with four trees in every row. The dots represent the positions of the trees that have been cut down.
problems.explanationZh: 插图显示了必须留下的十棵树，形成五行，每行四棵树。这些点代表被砍伐的树木的位置。

problems.promptEn: A gentleman wished to plant twenty-one trees in his park so that they should form twelve straight rows with five trees in every row. Could you have supplied him with a pretty symmetrical arrangement that would satisfy these conditions?
problems.promptZh: 一位绅士希望在他的公园里种植二十一棵树，以便 它们应该排成十二行，每行五棵树。你能为他提供一个相当对称的布置来满足这些条件吗？
problems.explanation: I give two pleasing arrangements of the trees. In each case there are twelve straight rows with five trees in every row.
problems.explanationEn: I give two pleasing arrangements of the trees. In each case there are twelve straight rows with five trees in every row.
problems.explanationZh: 我对树木做了两种令人愉快的排列。在每种情况下，都有十二行直行，每行有五棵树。

problems.promptEn: It will be seen in our illustration how twelve mince-pies may be placed on the table so as to form six straight rows with four pies in every row. The puzzle is to remove only four of them to new positions so that there shall be seven straight rows with four in every row. Which four would you remove, and where would you replace them? MOVING COUNTER PROBLEMS ‘I cannot do't without counters.' Winter's Tale, iv. 3.
problems.promptZh: 它将在我们的插图中看到 如何将十二个肉馅饼放在桌子上，形成六排直线，每排四个馅饼。难题是仅将其中的四个移至新位置，以便形成七排，每排四个。您会删除哪四个，以及在哪里替换它们？ 移动计数器问题“没有计数器我就不行。”冬天的故事，四。 3.
problems.explanation: If you ignore the four black pies in our illustration, the remaining twelve are in their original positions. Now remove the four detached pies to the places occupied by the black ones, and you will have your seven straight rows of four, as shown by the dotted lines.
problems.explanationEn: If you ignore the four black pies in our illustration, the remaining twelve are in their original positions. Now remove the four detached pies to the places occupied by the black ones, and you will have your seven straight rows of four, as shown by the dotted lines.
problems.explanationZh: 如果您忽略图中的四个黑色馅饼，则其余十二个馅饼都处于原来的位置。现在将四个分离的馅饼移至黑色馅饼占据的位置，您将得到七排直行，每排四个，如虚线所示。

problems.promptEn: It has been suggested that this puzzle was a great favourite among the young apprentices of the City of London in the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries. Readers will have noticed the curious brass grasshopper on the Royal Exchange. This long-lived creature escaped the fires of 1666 and 1838. The grasshopper, after his kind, was the crest of Sir Thomas Gresham, merchant grocer, who died in 1579, and from this cause it has been used as a sign by grocers in general. Unfortunately for the legend as to its origin, the puzzle was only produced by myself so late as the year 1900. On twelve of the thirteen black discs are placed numbered counters or grasshoppers. The puzzle is to reverse their order, so that they shall read, 1, 2, 3, 4, etc., in the opposite direction, with the vacant disc left in the same position as at present. Move one at a time in any order, either to the adjoining vacant disc or by jumping over one grasshopper, like the moves in draughts. The moves or leaps may be made in either direction that is at any time possible. What are the fewest possible moves in which it can be done? MOVING COUNTER PROBLEMS
problems.promptZh: 有人建议这个谜题很受欢迎 十六、十七世纪伦敦城的年轻学徒中。读者会注意到皇家交易所上那只好奇的黄铜蚱蜢。这种长寿的生物躲过了 1666 年和 1838 年的大火。蚱蜢是杂货商托马斯·格雷沙姆爵士 (Sir Thomas Gresham) 的徽章，他于 1579 年去世，因此它被一般杂货商用作标志。不幸的是，对于其起源的传说来说，这个谜题直到 1900 年才由我自己制作。 13 个黑色圆盘中的 12 个上放置了编号的计数器或蚱蜢。谜题是颠倒顺序，让他们以相反的方向读出 1、2、3、4 等，而空的圆盘则留在与当前位置相同的位置。以任意顺序一次移动一个，可以移动到相邻的空盘上，也可以跳过一只蚱蜢，就像跳棋中的移动一样。移动或跳跃可以在任何时间可能的任一方向上进行。最少可以完成哪些动作？ 移动柜台问题
problems.explanation: Move the counters in the following order. The moves in brackets are to be made four times in succession. 12, 1, 3, 2, 12, 11, 1, 3, 2 (5, 7, 9, 10, 8, 6, 4), 3, 2, 12, 11, 2, 1, 2. The grasshoppers will then be reversed in forty-four moves. The general solution of this problem is very difficult. Of course it can always be solved by the method given in the solution of the last puzzle, if we have no desire to use the fewest possible moves. But to employ a full economy of moves we have two main points to consider. There are always what I call a lower movement (L) and an upper movement (U). L consists in exchanging certain of Total No. of Counters L MOVEMENT U MOVEMENT Total No. of Moves No. of Counters No. of Moves No. of Counters No. of Moves 4n n – 1 and n 2(n – 1)² + 5n – 7 2n + 1 2n² + 3n +1 4(n² + n – 1) 4n – 2 n – 1 and n 2(n – 1)² + 5n – 7 2n – 1 2(n – 1)² + 3n – 2 4n² – 5 4n + 1 n and n + 1 2n² + 5n – 2 2n 2n² + 3n – 4 2(2n² + 4n – 3) 4n – 1 n – 1 and n 2(n – 1)² + 5n – 7 2n 2n² + 3n – 4 4n² + 4n – 9 Total No. of Counters L MOVEMENT U MOVEMENT Total No. of Moves No. of Counters No. of Moves No. of Counters No. of Moves 4n n and n 2n² + 3n – 4 2n 2(n – 1)² + 5n – 2 4(n² + n – 1) 4n – 2 n – 1 and n – 1 2(n – 1)² + 3n – 7 2n 2(n – 1)² + 5n – 2 4n² – 5 4n + 1 n and n 2n² + 3n – 4 2n + 1 2n² + 5n – 2 2(2n² + 4n – 3) 4n – 1 n and n 2n² + 3n – 4 2n – 1 2(n – 1)² + 5n – 7 4n² + 4n – 9 the highest numbers, such as 12, 11, 10 in our “Grasshopper Puzzle,” with certain of the lower numbers, 1, 2, 3; the former moving in a clockwise direction, the latter in a non-clockwise direction. U consists in reversing the intermediate counters. In the above solution for 12, it will be seen that 12, 11, and 1, 2, 3 are engaged in the L movement, and 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10 in the U movement. The L movement needs 16 moves and U 28, making together 44. We might also involve 10 in the L movement, which would result in L 23, U 21, making also together 44 moves. These I call the first and second methods. But any other scheme will entail an increase of moves. You always get these two methods (of equal economy) for odd or even counters, but the point is to determine just how many to involve in L and how many in U. Here is the solution in table form. But first note, in giving values to n, that 2, 3, and 4 counters are special cases, requiring respectively 3, 3, and 6 moves, and that 5 and 6 counters do not give a minimum solution by the second method—only by the first. More generally we may say that with m counters, where m is even and greater than 4, we require (m² + 4m) moves; and where m is odd and greater than 3, (m² + 6m) moves. I have thus shown the reader how to find the minimum number of moves for any case, and the character and direction of the moves. I will leave him to discover for himself how the actual order of moves is to be determined. This is a hard nut, and requires careful adjustment of the L and the U movements, so that they may be mutually accommodating.

The moves in brackets are to be made four times in succession. 12, 1, 3, 2, 12, 11, 1, 3, 2 (5, 7, 9, 10, 8, 6, 4), 3, 2, 12, 11, 2, 1, 2. The grasshoppers will then be reversed in forty-four moves. The general solution of this problem is very difficult. Of course it can always be solved by the method given in the solution of the last puzzle, if we have no desire to use the fewest possible moves. But to employ a full economy of moves we have two main points to consider. There are always what I call a lower movement (L) and an upper movement (U). The L movement needs 16 moves and U 28, making together 44. We might also involve 10 in the L movement, which would result in L 23, U 21, making also together 44 moves. These I call the first and second methods. But any other scheme will entail an increase of moves. You always get these two methods (of equal economy) for odd or even counters, but the point is to determine just how many to involve in L and how many in U. Here is the solution in table form. More generally we may say that with m counters, where m is even and greater than 4, we require (m² + 4m) moves; and where m is odd and greater than 3, (m² + 6m) moves.
